scene_title: Another part of the same street, before the house of Brutus

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I prithee, boy, run to the senate-house;

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Stay not to answer me, but get thee gone:

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Why dost thou stay?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: To know my errand, madam.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I would have had thee there, and here again,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Ere I can tell thee what thou shouldst do there.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: O constancy, be strong upon my side,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Set a huge mountain ’tween my heart and tongue!

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I have a man’s mind, but a woman’s might.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: How hard it is for women to keep counsel!

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Art thou here yet?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Madam, what should I do?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Run to the Capitol, and nothing else?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: And so return to you, and nothing else?

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Yes, bring me word, boy, if thy lord look well,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: For he went sickly forth: and take good note

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: What Caesar doth, what suitors press to him.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Hark, boy! what noise is that?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: I hear none, madam.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Prithee, listen well;

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I heard a bustling rumour, like a fray,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And the wind brings it from the Capitol.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Sooth, madam, I hear nothing.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Come hither, fellow: which way hast thou been?

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: At mine own house, good lady.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: What is’t o’clock?

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: About the ninth hour, lady.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Is Caesar yet gone to the Capitol?

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Madam, not yet: I go to take my stand,

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: To see him pass on to the Capitol.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Thou hast some suit to Caesar, hast thou not?

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: That I have, lady: if it will please Caesar

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: To be so good to Caesar as to hear me,

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: I shall beseech him to befriend himself.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Why, know’st thou any harm’s intended towards him?

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: None that I know will be, much that I fear may chance.

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Good morrow to you. Here the street is narrow:

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: The throng that follows Caesar at the heels,

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Of senators, of praetors, common suitors,

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Will crowd a feeble man almost to death:

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: I’ll get me to a place more void, and there

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Speak to great Caesar as he comes along.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: I must go in. Ay me, how weak a thing

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: The heart of woman is! O Brutus,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: The heavens speed thee in thine enterprise!

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Sure, the boy heard me: Brutus hath a suit

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: That Caesar will not grant. O, I grow faint.

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Run, Lucius, and commend me to my lord;

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: Say I am merry: come to me again,

lines.speaker: PORTIA
lines.text: And bring me word what he doth say to thee.

glosses.phrase: O constancy, be strong upon my side
glosses.definition: Portia invokes constancy — the Stoic virtue of steadfastness, her claim (2.1) and lineage (Cato's daughter) — to hold her tongue

glosses.phrase: man's mind, but a woman's might
glosses.definition: a man's intelligence but a woman's (smaller) strength — Portia's gendered self-description; the play treats gender as a limit on constancy
a noise of commotion, like a scuffle
about 9 a.m. — the Roman and Elizabethan hour (sunrise was the first hour)
beg him to act in his own interest — the Soothsayer's second warning, now obliquely stated
Roman magistrates, ranked below consuls; Brutus held a praetorship in 44 BCE
Brutus has a petition (Portia knows the truth; she disguises her outburst as concern for a business matter)
carry my greetings to my husband

annotations.title: Portia after the secret — the cost of inclusion
annotations.body:
Portia in 2.1 demanded to share her husband's burden; 2.4 shows what that sharing cost her. Having been told the conspiracy, she cannot eat, cannot sleep, cannot control her speech ('How hard it is for women to keep counsel!'). She sends Lucius on a vague errand because she cannot be alone with the knowledge. Her Stoic training — 'O constancy, be strong upon my side' — buckles under a specific load: the political knowledge she insisted on having.

Shakespeare does not frame this as a failure of Portia's character. He frames it as what the play has not told us before — that Stoic fortitude is a practice, not a possession, and that political knowledge imposes a real cost on whoever holds it. The scene's pathos comes from our knowing (as Portia does not yet) that her anxiety is about to be vindicated. By 4.3 we will learn Portia has killed herself by swallowing fire; the destination is already visible in this scene.

The Soothsayer's return — now fearing for Caesar without specific knowledge — completes the scene. Two figures, one knowing, one not, both waiting for the same thing to happen. The waiting is the subject.
sources: Linda Woodbridge, *Women and the English Renaissance* (1984)

trials.prompt: What does Portia's 2.4 scene show about the cost of having been told the conspiracy?
options: That Stoic fortitude buckles under the specific load of political knowledge; she cannot eat, sleep, or control her speech, and the strain foreshadows her suicide in 4.3
That she has forgotten the secret
That she is planning to betray it

trials.prompt: Why does Portia send Lucius on a vague errand to the Capitol?
options: To get him out of her hair
Because she cannot bear to be alone with the secret; the errand is a way of outsourcing her anxiety
To carry a message to Brutus
